system.description: This rugged leather backpack is fitted with multiple buckled pockets and a rolled blanket strapped across the top, showing the scuffs and creases of long travel. It is typically used by adventurers to carry rations, tools, and gear on the road, keeping their hands free for weapons, torches, or climbing.

This bedroll is a tightly rolled bundle of thick, worn canvas or wool, secured with a pair of cracked leather straps and brass buckles. It is typically used by travelers and adventurers as a simple portable sleeping mat, providing a little warmth and comfort when camping under the open sky or on dungeon stone.

This large belt pouch is a rounded, roomy leather bag with a broad flap and brass-buckled strap, built to hang securely from a waistbelt. It is typically used by adventurers to carry bulkier essentials like rations, tools, or extra coin—when a small pouch simply won’t suffice. 1 pouch can be attached to a single belt.
A small leather pouch that attaches to a belt. 2 pouches can be attached to a single belt.

This is a compact, square leather bolt case designed to attach securely to a belt. It is typically used by crossbowmen to carry and protect up to thirty bolts, keeping them accessible and organized during travel or battle.
An iron bar, 2' long. Used for prying open doors, chest lids, etc.
A bunch of garlic, sometimes used to repel undead creatures.

Made of iron, with 3-4 hooks and a ring to attach a rope. Designed to be thrown by hand and attached to 50 ft. of rope (~6 lbs if hemp rope). The combined 'grappling hook + rope' kit typically weighs about 9 lbs total.

Comparable to a carpenter's or small blacksmith's hammer light enough for one-handed use, but heavy enough to drive spikes or nails. Besides hammering in iron spikes, it is also useful for tapping stonework.

A religious emblem, amulet, or pendant (metal, wood, or stone)Usually worn on a chain or leather thong. Even ornate silver or gem-inlaid versions rarely exceed ½ lb. A large ceremonial one (altar piece) could weigh several pounds, but the adventuring version is light and portable.
Water blessed by a holy person, stored in a special vial for use in rituals. Inflicts damage on undead when used as a weapon. A vial is easily held in one hand or stored in a pouch.
Used for wedging doors open or shut, anchoring ropes, and various dungeon uses. 6-8 inches long, about ½ inch thick. Typically carried in a small leather pouch or tool roll.

A bullseye lantern is a metal-cased lamp with a shuttered housing and a single, thick glass lens that focuses its light into a narrow, far-reaching beam. Adventurers typically use it when they need to see a long distance down corridors or roads while keeping surrounding areas in comparative shadow. It burns one flask of oil per 4 hours (24 turns) and casts light in a 60' cone.

A hooded lantern is a metal-framed light source with a directional hood that shields the flame and focuses its beam forward. It is commonly used by travelers and adventurers who need controlled, wind-resistant illumination in dark or hazardous environments. Burns one flask of oil per 4 hours (24 turns). Casts light in a 30' radius.
Durable and unbreakable compared to glass. Commonly used for signaling, checking around corners, or personal grooming. Often stored in a thin leather sleeve or wooden case.
Durable and unbreakable compared to glass. Commonly used for signaling, checking around corners, or personal grooming. Often stored in a thin leather sleeve or wooden case.
Used to fuel lanterns or as a weapon when ignited. Burns for 4 hours in a lantern or 1 turn when used to create a pool of fire. Often carried in a padded or leather-lined pouch to prevent breakage.

This metal piton is a tapered spike with a forged eyelet for threading rope, its surface worn and scuffed from repeated climbing use. Adventurers hammer it into cracks or seams in stone to create secure anchor points for climbing, rappelling, and traversing dangerous terrain.

A 10-foot wooden pole used for prodding suspicious areas or measuring distances in a dungeon. Carried with difficulty indoors or underground; too long to strap to a pack, usually dragged or carried in hand. May be split into two 5-ft. sections for easier transport.
Worn on the back, an arrow quiver is a cylindrical leather container designed to carry and protect up to 20 arrows while keeping them easily accessible during travel or combat.
A compact quiver worn on the hip and meant to hang from the belt. It holds 12 or fewer arrows.
Food for long journeys or expeditions. Non-perishable, waterproof, and compact but heavy and bland. Often divided into daily pouches for easy rationing.

Fresh, unpreserved food suitable for shorter trips. Tastier but bulkier than iron rations. Cannot be carried indefinitely without spoilage unless kept cool or magically preserved. Common among caravan travelers or short expeditions.

Hempen rope is a thick, coarse rope made from natural plant fibers, valued for its strength and reliability. A 50-foot length is commonly used for climbing, hauling gear, binding objects, or securing camps and equipment in the field.

Silken rope is a finely woven, lightweight yet strong cordage prized for its smooth texture and durability. Adventurers commonly use a 50-foot length for climbing, securing loads, setting traps, or safely descending into pits and ruins.

This large sack is a coarse burlap bag tied shut with a thick rope, its fabric worn from heavy use and bulging with whatever has been stuffed inside. Adventurers typically use it to haul bulky loot, provisions, or miscellaneous gear that won't fit neatly into a pack. Capacity: 24 slots (600 coins).

This small sack is a soft burlap pouch cinched tight with a simple cord, its rough fibers worn from frequent handling. Adventurers typically use it to store rations, coins, or loose gear that needs to be kept separate inside a pack. Capacity: 8 slots (200 coins).

A leather sheath worn on the belt designed to carry a single one-handed sword or similarly sized blade. Keeps the weapon protected and ready to draw in combat. A scabbard occupies one hip slot on a belt.

A wide leather back-sheath worn across the shoulders, designed to carry a two-handed sword or large weapon with the hilt accessible over the shoulder. Frees the hands while keeping a large blade secure and ready.

A protective tube designed to store and transport parchment scrolls, maps, or important documents. Crafted from sturdy materials like wood, leather, and metal, it shields its contents from weather and wear while allowing adventurers or scholars to carry their writings safely on the road.

Picks, tension wrench(s), small pliers, razor file, awl, miniature mirror on a stick, small oiler, spare springs/pins, leather roll or wooden/metal case. Used by thieves to open locks and disable traps.

This flint and tinder kit includes a sharp-edged flint stone, a curved steel striker, and a bundled nest of dry fibers for catching sparks. Adventurers use it to start campfires, ignite torches, and light oil in situations where magical fire is unavailable.
A bundle of six torches, wooden shafts wrapped with oil-soaked rags. Each burns for 1 hour (6 turns) and casts light in a 30' radius. An individual torch can also serve as an improvised melee weapon.

This small glass vial is a sturdy, cork-sealed container used to hold potions, holy water, alchemical mixtures, or other valuable liquids. Adventurers and scholars alike rely on such vials to store and transport substances safely while keeping them readily accessible in the field.

A waterskin is a durable leather container used to carry drinking water while traveling or adventuring. It is lightweight, easy to strap to a belt or pack, and essential for surviving long journeys where fresh water is scarce.
Stoppered glass bottle, clay jug, or wineskin containing two pints of table or fortified wine.

A flowering herb that is highly toxic if ingested or absorbed through open skin. Used in alchemy, rituals, or to repel lycanthropes. Loses potency after a few weeks unless kept dried and sealed. Must be struck in melee to be effective.

This wooden spike is a rough-hewn stake with one end whittled to a sharp point and the other left blunt for hammering. It's typically used to secure tents and ropes, mark trails, or improvise simple traps and defenses in the field.
